focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The candle burned the way candles burned. For the past two weeks Gavin had come down night after night with the manuscript and worked the same three-line exercise he'd decoded weeks ago: perceive the agreement governing the candle's flame, hold the perception steady, and adjust. *Adjust* was the word he used because the manuscript's actual term sat somewhere between *renegotiate* and *edit* and *speak differently to* — three English words where the original used one symbol that contained all of them and none of them exactly.

He sat on the stone floor of the sub-collection with his back against the wall, the manuscript open across his lap. The room was cold — December cold, the deep mineral kind that came up through the stone itself and settled into his legs and the base of his spine. The sub-collection was always colder than the rest of the castle, and this deep into winter the chill had a permanence to it, as if the limestone had made a final decision about temperature and would not be moved. The room's own light — faint, steady, a luminescence from the walls that had nothing to do with torches or spells — was enough to read by. But the exercise needed a target. Something small whose rules he could see.

He could see them. That was the part that made him want to put his head through the wall. The flame's flicker was built on agreements — heat and air and fuel and the specific relationship between combustion and the magical substrate that kept fire in a magical building burning by the rules wizards expected. He could see the edges of those agreements now, dim and structural, layered beneath the visible flame like a second architecture. When he let his focus settle past the light, the substrate showed itself as pressure, rules being applied, deep-water weig...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...s the terrible crayon cat face-down on the table beside her textbook. The feather rose. It hung. It fell. She put it back and this time, when it would not go cleanly, she leaned toward the girl beside her and asked something Gavin couldn't hear.

Gavin looked at the two and a half lines in his notebook and shut it. He slid the notebook back into his bag. There was no version of them he could say to Miren tomorrow that would not sound mad, and no version he could hand to Babbling that would come with proof attached, but that was not the whole of it now. He could still see the little crease that had appeared between Ffion's eyebrows when he'd nearly said too much. He did not want another first-year looking at him like that because he couldn't keep a feather as a feather in his own head for five minutes. He didn't go back to the sub-collection. Not tonight. Tomorrow night the manuscript would be there, in the same small cold room, in the same patient notation that kept rearranging itself for a reader it hadn't been designed for. The rest of the questions — what the wards protected against, why the Builders had thought a planet needed hiding, what existed outside the wards that made concealment worth the work of a civilization that could reshape reality as a medium of thought — would wait in the pages he hadn't reached, in the slow configurations of symbols that showed things his mind couldn't quite resolve, in the quiet of a room lit by a light that a vanished civilization had left on when they stepped through a door that wasn't a door into whatever they'd become.

He closed his eyes. The fire was warm. Across the room, when the feather dipped again, Ffion turned to the girl beside her instead of to him. Gavin let her. The feather rose, settled, and rose again without him.
